summary: Gruber reviews Deep Green, Joachim Bondo's chess game for iPhone, noting it's a complete rewrite of Bondo's celebrated Newton chess app from 1998. He praises its minimalist UI design, polish, and attention to detail as exemplifying ideal iPhone app design. The app offers the same functionality as competitors but with far less UI complexity, which took four extra months to achieve. Gruber highlights Deep Green as proof that simpler interfaces require more effort but produce better results.
key_points: Deep Green is a complete rewrite of the best Newton chess app, not a port, maintaining the same design philosophy across a decade
The app exemplifies Gruber's iPhone UI rule: do the absolute minimum needed, then polish the hell out of the experience
Bondo spent four extra months beyond competitors specifically to achieve a simpler, more minimal UI
The interaction design supports both drag-and-drop and tap-to-move, showing thoughtful consideration of touch input
money_quotes: Figure out the absolute least you need to do to implement the idea, do just that, and then polish the hell out of the experience.
It takes more time and more effort to make software with a simpler UI, but it's worth it.
Deep Green offers pretty much the same functionality as the rest, and sometimes more, but with a fraction of the UI. Achieving this is why I'm 4 months later than the rest.
It looks and feels exactly how an iPhone chess game should.
I first released Deep Green 10 years ago, in April 1998, for Apple's Newton. In a matter of days hereafter, Steve Jobs terminated the Newton altogether.
Somehow I don't think he'll have that problem with the iPhone.
key_insight: The best software comes from doing less with more effort — achieving simplicity in UI requires more time and discipline than adding complexity, but the result is worth the wait.
